title: practical english usage - Part 351
content:
Seem to be is normal before noun phrases.

She seems (to be) very sleepy today.

- She doesn't seem to be ready yet.

suggest that he might actually be older than he is.) not He seems older than he is. (     He- seems to -be-older-than he-is - this would years older than the rest.

- According to the experts, the north side of the castle seems to be about 100 vocabulary •  579  since: tenses It was ages since that wonderful trip.

It's a long time since the last meeting.

It is! was... since...

In British English, present and past tenses are common in the structure 2  It's  a  long     time     since      ...

Things weren't going so well since Father's illness.

Since last Sunday I can't stop thinking about you.

She doesn't come round to see us so much  since her marriage.

You're looking much  better since your operation.

sentences about changes.

However, present and past tenses are also occasionally found, especially in university days.

I was sorry when Jacky moved to America; we had been good friends since We haven't seen Jamie since Christmas.

I've known her since 2005. (     J know-her since...) past perfect tenses in the main clause.

In sentences with since (referring to time), we  normally use present perfect and 1  main    clause:    I've  known        her    since      . . .

579 since:     tenses ►    491 and sick        ill ►    422 and shut        dose There's an old story about a man without a shadow.

In the evening your shadow is longer than you are.

A  shadow is the ‘ picture’  made by something that blocks out light.

The temperature's 30 degrees in the shade.

I'm hot. Let's find some shade to sit in.

Shade is protection from the sun.

578  shade       and   shado      w Sensible is a ‘ false friend’  - similar words in some languages mean ‘ sensitive’ .

Have you got a sun cream for sensitive skin? (     .. .ffn-sensible^kiH?) not Don't shout at her - she's very sensitive, (     . . . very sensible.) A  sensitive person feels things easily or deeply, and may be easily hurt.

7 want to buy that dress/ ‘ Be sensible, dear. It's much  too expensive/ A  sensible person has common sense, and does not make stupid decisions.

577 sensible         and   sensitive Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  580  small and little For little used as a determiner meaning ‘ not much ’  (e.g.  There's little hope), ►     168.

(More natural than ... the littlest baby...) He's the smallest baby I've ever seen.

The puppy was so little.. .) The puppy was so small and sweet. (More natural in British English than and comparative and superlative forms are not normally used.

In British English little is unusual in 'predicative' position (after a verb), the little hand of a clock a little way little finger         a little while In a few fixed expressions, little is used in the same way as small or short, They've bought a pretty little house in the country.

What's that nasty little boy doing in our garden?

'What's he like? 'Oh, he's aJunny little man.' Poor little thing - come here and let me look after you.

The  adjective little usually expresses some kind of emotion.

You're too small to be a police officer.

Could I have a small brandy, please?

Small simply refers to size. It is the opposite of big or large (►  401).

580 small      and   little ►     377 and since,     as,   because            for 469 and           time         ► since,     for,   in      from: For since meaning ‘ as’  or ‘ because’ , ►    377.

For past perfect tenses, ►    53  55.   For the differences between since, for and from, ►    469.

For more about present perfect tenses, including American usage,  ►     I"  52.

We visit my parents every week since we've had the car.

- We visit my parents every week since we bought the car.

You've drunk about ten cups of tea since you've been sitting here.

- You've drunk about ten cups of tea since you arrived.

I've known her since I've lived in this street.

- I've known her since we were at school together.

The  tense in the s/nce-clause can be perfect or past, depending on the meaning.
